extra.message: User-controlled input from request.args.get('name') at sandboxes/views.py:13 is rendered with the | safe Jinja2 filter, bypassing auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript via the ?name= query parameter, e.g. ?name=<script>alert(1)</script>.

User-controlled input from request.cookies.get('name') or request.form.get('name') at sandboxes/views.py:27-28 is rendered with the | safe Jinja2 filter, bypassing auto-escaping. An attacker can set a malicious cookie or POST form data to execute arbitrary JavaScript.

Database-stored message.from_user and message.message fields are rendered with the | safe filter at lines 29-30. User-controlled data can be injected into these fields via the unauthenticated API endpoint at api/views.py:43-56 (MessagesView.post()) or the authenticated feedback form at account_area/views.py:75. When an admin views messages, stored XSS payloads will execute in the admin's browser.

The logged-in user's username (g.user.username) is rendered with the | safe Jinja2 filter in the base template. If a user's username contains a malicious payload (settable via the admin panel's CRUD interface through AdminOnlyModelView), every page view for that user will execute the stored XSS payload.

The 'next' query parameter from user input is passed directly to Flask's redirect() function without validation. An attacker can craft a URL that redirects users to an arbitrary external site, enabling phishing attacks. This endpoint lacks proper URL validation or an allowlist of safe redirect targets.

cwe: CWE-601: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')

extra.message: Passwords are stored and compared in plaintext. User.password at models/models.py:11 is compared directly with the submitted password using == at login/views.py:17. There is no password hashing (bcrypt, argon2, etc.) used. If the database is compromised, all credentials are exposed in cleartext.

cwe: CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information

extra.message: The Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as a plaintext string 'Keep it secret, keep it safe'. This allows an attacker to forge session cookies, decrypt session data, and potentially escalate privileges. The secret key should be loaded from environment variables or a secure configuration system.

Flask DEBUG mode is permanently enabled (app.config['DEBUG'] = True). When an error occurs, the Werkzeug debugger will display detailed stack traces, source code, and an interactive console, potentially leaking sensitive information including environment variables, source code, and database credentials.

The User.serialize property (lines 24-29) exposes the 'password' field in serialized output. If this property is used in any API response (e.g., through the sqlalchemy admin views or any future endpoint), user passwords would be leaked to unauthorized parties.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The MessagesView.post() method (lines 43-56) has no authentication decorator (@admin_required or @login_required), unlike all other methods in the class. Any unauthenticated user can POST arbitrary messages to the server. Combined with the stored XSS in admin/message.html:29-30, an attacker can inject malicious messages that will execute JavaScript when viewed by an administrator.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
